title: Rice and peas
subtitle: Jamaican kidney bean rice in coconut milk
excerpt: Rice and peas is the essential side dish of Jamaican cooking: long-grain rice cooked in coconut milk with kidney beans, thyme, scotch bonnet, and allspice until each grain is separate and fragrant. Despite the name, the peas are kidney beans — and the coconut milk is not optional.
sourceNotes:
• Levi Roots, *Reggae Reggae Cookbook* (2008). Principal reference for the coconut milk ratio and the whole scotch bonnet flavouring technique.
• Riaz Phillips, *Belly Full: Caribbean Food in the UK* (2021). Cross-reference for the British-Caribbean home kitchen proportions and the kidney bean method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Rice and peas is best made fresh. Leftovers keep for 2 days in the fridge and reheat well with a splash of water.

content.text: Rice and peas is served alongside jerk chicken, brown stew chicken, or curry goat in Jamaican kitchens — it is less a side dish than a given. The name is misleading: the peas are kidney beans, cooked in coconut milk with a whole scotch bonnet that stays intact throughout. The chilli flavours the coconut milk without breaking and releasing its heat, so the finished rice is fragrant rather than hot.

The ratio of liquid matters. Too much and the rice turns sticky; too little and the bottom scorches before the grains are cooked through. Coconut milk is thicker than water, so the total liquid needs adjusting downward from a plain rice recipe. The method below gives separate, fragrant grains that hold their shape.
A medium saucepan with a tight-fitting lid is essential — steam does the final cooking. If the lid is loose, cover with foil before putting the lid on to trap the steam.

Pour {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk into a medium saucepan. Add 200 ml of cold water, {{kidney-beans}} of drained kidney beans, {{thyme}} thyme sprigs, {{scotch-bonnet}} whole scotch bonnet, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{allspice}} of allspice, {{spring-onion}} whole spring onions, and {{salt-table}} of salt. Bring to the boil over a medium heat.

Stir in {{long-grain-rice}} of rinsed rice. Bring back to the boil, then reduce the heat to the lowest setting. Cover tightly and cook for 20 minutes. Do not lift the lid during cooking — the steam is what finishes the grains.

After 20 minutes, remove from the heat and leave covered for 5 minutes. Lift the lid: the rice should be cooked through with no pooled liquid at the bottom. Remove and discard the scotch bonnet, thyme sprigs, spring onions, and garlic before serving. Fluff with a fork.

items.cause: The rice was not rinsed, or the heat was too high and the liquid over-reduced before the rice cooked through.
items.fix: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear before cooking — this removes excess surface starch. Keep the heat at the lowest possible setting once the lid is on.

items.symptom: Rice is undercooked with liquid remaining
items.cause: The lid let steam escape, or the heat was too low to maintain a simmer.
items.fix: If liquid remains and the rice is still hard, cover with foil under the lid and cook for another 5 minutes. Add a tablespoon of hot water if the pan looks dry.

items.symptom: Rice is too hot to eat
items.cause: The scotch bonnet burst during cooking, releasing its seeds.
items.fix: Use the whole, uncut chilli and handle it gently — do not pierce or press it. If it bursts, remove immediately and stir the rice to distribute the heat before tasting.

items.symptom: Coconut flavour is not coming through
items.cause: Low-fat coconut milk or coconut drink was used instead of full-fat tinned coconut milk.
items.fix: Full-fat tinned coconut milk is essential. Shake the tin well before opening — the cream and liquid can separate.

content.text: Rice and peas is eaten every Sunday in Jamaican households and on any occasion where the main course is a Jamaican braise or grill. In Britain it came with the Caribbean community and has never left: it is made in home kitchens from London to Birmingham every week. The name comes from the Jamaican use of "peas" for beans — a term that maps to gungo peas (pigeon peas) at Christmas and kidney beans at other times. The coconut milk is what makes it specifically Jamaican rather than simply rice with beans.
